sources.sourceName: OpenStax Psychology 2e, Ch. 7 Review Questions (Thinking and Intelligence) and Ch. 8 Review Questions (Memory)
sources.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)

sources.sourceName: OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (answer key for odd-numbered review questions)

meta.notes: Psychology (PSBB) items scraped verbatim from OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch. 7 and Ch. 8 review-questions pages; correct answers from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), odd-numbered review questions only. Ch. 8 Q1: the Solutions Guide prints the answer as 'short-term memory' but the live 2e option C reads 'working memory' (Baddeley's model); the keyed letter C is unchanged and 'working memory' is the accurate term.

hints.prompt: The root word 'cognition' refers to which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mental processes such as thinking, knowing, and remembering

choices.label: B
choices.text: The physical growth of the body

hints.rationale: Cognition refers to mental processes such as thinking.

hints.prompt: Which subfield focuses on how people grow and change across the lifespan?
hints.rationale: Developmental psychology studies lifespan growth and change, not thinking, so 'human development' can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because one traditionally studies only observable behavior and the other studies how people influence one another in groups - neither focusing specifically on internal thinking?
hints.rationale: The study of observable behavior and of social influence are distinct from the study of internal thought.

questions.stem: Cognitive psychology is the branch of psychology that focuses on the study of ________.
questions.explanation: Cognitive psychology is the field that studies human thinking, including perception, language, attention, problem solving, and memory.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q1 = B.

hints.prompt: In cognitive psychology, an 'artificial concept' is defined by what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A specific, fixed set of rules or defining features

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fuzzy features learned gradually from everyday experience

choices.label: D
choices.text: A random collection of items with no definition

hints.rationale: An artificial concept is defined by a precise set of rules.

hints.prompt: A 'natural concept,' by contrast, is best described as one that is which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Formed through everyday experience and lacking rigid defining rules

choices.label: B
choices.text: Defined by a precise mathematical formula

hints.rationale: Natural concepts are experience-based and fuzzy, so experience-based categories are not 'artificial.'

hints.prompt: Which two options are clearly natural concepts because they are broad categories learned informally from experience?
hints.rationale: Mammals and teachers are broad, experience-based categories, so both can be eliminated as artificial concepts.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of an artificial concept?
questions.explanation: An artificial concept is defined by a specific set of rules or features. A triangle's area (1/2 base x height) is defined by a precise mathematical rule, making it an artificial concept; natural concepts like mammals develop from experience.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q3 = B.

hints.prompt: The four options are all terms from the study of which of the following?
hints.rationale: All four are terms from linguistics, the study of language.

hints.prompt: Semantics deals with which aspect of language?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The meaning of words and phrases

choices.label: B
choices.text: The rules for ordering words into sentences

choices.label: D
choices.text: The influence of language on thought

hints.rationale: Semantics concerns meaning, not the arrangement of words into sentences, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because one is simply a language's vocabulary (its set of words) and the other is the hypothesis that language shapes thought - neither being about arranging words into sentences?
hints.rationale: A lexicon is a vocabulary and linguistic determinism is about language shaping thought, so neither governs sentence structure.

questions.stem: ________ provides general principles for organizing words into meaningful sentences.
questions.explanation: Syntax refers to the rules and principles for organizing words into grammatically correct, meaningful sentences.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q5 = D.

hints.prompt: This question asks which set of rules governs the MEANING of language. Meaning is which aspect of language?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Its semantic content - what words and phrases refer to

choices.label: B
choices.text: The pitch of the speaker's voice

choices.label: C
choices.text: The number of words spoken per minute

hints.rationale: Meaning corresponds to the semantic content of language.

hints.prompt: Phonemes are the building blocks of language that represent what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The smallest distinct units of speech SOUND

choices.label: D
choices.text: The grammatical rules of a language

hints.rationale: Phonemes are units of sound, not meaning, so they can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because one is a language's vocabulary and the other is a grammatical error children make (such as saying 'goed' for 'went') - neither being the rules of meaning?
hints.rationale: A lexicon is vocabulary and overgeneralization is a grammatical error pattern, so neither determines meaning.

questions.stem: The meaning of words and phrases is determined by applying the rules of ________.
questions.explanation: Semantics is the set of rules governing the meaning of words and phrases in a language.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q7 = D.

hints.prompt: The four options are all approaches to which cognitive task?
hints.rationale: Algorithms, heuristics, mental sets, and trial and error are all problem-solving approaches.

hints.prompt: A heuristic differs from a precise step-by-step formula in that a heuristic is which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A mental shortcut or rule of thumb that is fast but not guaranteed to work

choices.label: B
choices.text: A precise procedure that always yields the correct answer

choices.label: C
choices.text: An inherited reflex present at birth

choices.label: D
choices.text: A type of long-term memory store

hints.rationale: A heuristic is a fast shortcut that does not guarantee a solution, unlike a precise formula, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because one is the tendency to stick with previously successful approaches and the other is trying possibilities more or less at random - neither being a guaranteed step-by-step formula?

choices.label: A
choices.text: mental set and trial and error

hints.rationale: A mental set is a habitual approach and trial and error is unsystematic guessing, so neither is a precise formula.

questions.stem: A specific formula for solving a problem is called ________.
questions.explanation: An algorithm is a specific, step-by-step formula or procedure that, if followed correctly, guarantees a solution to a problem.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q9 = A.

hints.prompt: The four options are all examples of what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cognitive biases that can distort judgment and decision-making

hints.rationale: Anchoring, confirmation, representativeness, and availability are all cognitive biases.

hints.prompt: Confirmation bias is the tendency to do which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Search for and favor information that confirms what you already believe

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rely too heavily on the first piece of information you receive

choices.label: C
choices.text: Judge likelihood by how easily examples come to mind

choices.label: D
choices.text: Judge by how well something matches a prototype

hints.rationale: Confirmation bias is favoring belief-consistent information, not fixating on a single trait, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because one judges probability by similarity to a prototype and the other by how easily examples come to mind - neither being 'fixating on one trait'?
hints.rationale: Representativeness relies on prototype similarity and availability on ease of recall, so neither is fixation on a single trait.

questions.stem: Which type of bias involves becoming fixated on a single trait of a problem?
questions.explanation: Anchoring bias is the tendency to focus on (anchor to) one particular piece of information or trait when making decisions.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q11 = A.

hints.prompt: Psychologists contrast 'fluid' intelligence with 'crystallized' intelligence. This distinction divides intelligence into which two aspects?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The ability to reason with novel problems versus accumulated knowledge and facts

choices.label: D
choices.text: The sense of vision versus the sense of hearing

hints.rationale: Fluid intelligence is reasoning with novel problems; crystallized intelligence is accumulated knowledge.

hints.prompt: Crystallized intelligence is best demonstrated by which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recalling learned facts, vocabulary, and accumulated knowledge

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reasoning through a brand-new puzzle you have never seen

choices.label: D
choices.text: Adapting successfully to a foreign culture

hints.rationale: Crystallized intelligence is recalling accumulated knowledge, so 'recalling information' reflects crystallized, not fluid, intelligence.

hints.prompt: Creating new products and successfully navigating different cultures are better described as which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Creative and practical intelligence, rather than fluid reasoning

hints.rationale: Making new products reflects creative intelligence and cross-cultural skill reflects practical intelligence, so both can be eliminated as definitions of fluid intelligence.

choices.label: B
choices.text: being able to create new products

choices.label: C
choices.text: being able to understand and communicate with different cultures

choices.label: D
choices.text: being able to see complex relationships and solve problems

questions.explanation: Fluid intelligence is the capacity to reason, see complex relationships, and solve novel problems, independent of acquired knowledge (crystallized intelligence).
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q13 = D.

hints.prompt: The term 'triarchic' indicates that this theory divides intelligence into how many components?
hints.rationale: 'Tri-' means three, so the triarchic theory has three components.

hints.prompt: Howard Gardner is best known for which theory of intelligence?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The theory of multiple intelligences (musical, spatial, bodily-kinesthetic, and others)

hints.rationale: Gardner is known for multiple intelligences, not the triarchic theory, so he can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Daniel Goleman is most associated with popularizing which concept?
hints.rationale: Goleman is tied to emotional intelligence, not the triarchic theory, and 'Steitz' is not a known intelligence theorist, so both can be eliminated.

questions.stem: Which theorist put forth the triarchic theory of intelligence?
questions.explanation: Robert Sternberg proposed the triarchic theory of intelligence, comprising practical, creative, and analytical intelligence.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q15 = C.

hints.prompt: Standardizing and 'norming' a test means establishing what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A consistent testing procedure and a set of reference scores for comparison

choices.label: B
choices.text: The retail price of the test booklet

choices.label: C
choices.text: The color of the answer sheet

choices.label: D
choices.text: The personal opinion of the test's author

hints.rationale: Norming establishes reference standards so scores can be compared.

hints.prompt: Why is norming a test on only 'children of average intelligence' inadequate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because the test must be valid across the FULL range of ability, not just the average

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because average-ability children are unable to take tests

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because intelligence cannot be measured at all

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because only adults can ever be used for norming

hints.rationale: Norming only on average-ability children ignores the full ability range, so that option can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because each names only a narrow SUBGROUP rather than a cross-section of the whole population?

choices.label: A
choices.text: a group of same-age peers and children with mental disabilities

choices.label: B
choices.text: a representative sample and same-age peers

choices.label: C
choices.text: children with mental disabilities and a representative sample

choices.label: D
choices.text: children of average intelligence and a representative sample

hints.rationale: Same-age peers and children with disabilities are each narrow subgroups, not a full cross-section of the population.

questions.stem: In order for a test to be normed and standardized it must be tested on ________.
questions.explanation: Norming and standardizing a test requires administering it to a representative sample of the population so that scores can be meaningfully compared.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q17 = B.

hints.prompt: The four options are all figures associated with the history of what?
hints.rationale: All four names are associated with the history of intelligence testing.

hints.prompt: Alfred Binet is credited with creating which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the first practical intelligence tests, to identify schoolchildren needing help

choices.label: B
choices.text: The modern adult Wechsler intelligence scales

choices.label: D
choices.text: The concept of heritability of traits

hints.rationale: Binet built an early children's intelligence test that others later adapted, which distinguishes it from the most widely used test today.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because one pioneered early hereditary measurements of ability and the other REVISED an existing French test into the Stanford-Binet?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sir Francis Galton and Louis Terman

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sir Francis Galton and David Wechsler

hints.rationale: Galton focused on hereditary measurement and Terman adapted Binet's test into the Stanford-Binet, so neither developed today's most widely used IQ test.

questions.stem: Who developed the IQ test most widely used today?
questions.explanation: David Wechsler developed the Wechsler scales (e.g., the WAIS), the most widely used individual IQ tests today.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q19 = D.

hints.prompt: The debate over whether traits come from genes or from experience is known as which classic issue in psychology?
hints.rationale: Genes versus experience is the nature-versus-nurture debate.

hints.prompt: Evidence that identical twins raised APART still show similar intelligence suggests which factor plays a real role?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Only the environment, with no role for genes

hints.rationale: Twins-raised-apart similarities show genetics matters, so an answer of 'neither' would be wrong.

hints.prompt: Studies showing that enriched schooling and good nutrition can raise measured intelligence demonstrate the importance of which factor?
hints.rationale: Environmental enrichment affecting intelligence shows environment matters too, so 'neither A nor B' can be eliminated and a genetics-only view is incomplete.

questions.stem: Where does high intelligence come from?
questions.explanation: Intelligence arises from an interaction of both genetics and environment; neither alone fully accounts for it.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q21 = C.

hints.prompt: A learning disability specifically affects a person's ability to do which of the following, despite otherwise normal intelligence?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acquire particular academic skills such as reading or math

hints.rationale: A learning disability affects specific academic skills despite normal overall intelligence.

hints.prompt: How does a learning disability differ from an intellectual disability?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A learning disability affects specific skills while overall intelligence is typically normal

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are exactly the same condition

choices.label: C
choices.text: A learning disability means overall IQ is very low

choices.label: D
choices.text: A learning disability is purely a matter of emotions

hints.rationale: A learning disability is specific with normal overall intelligence, unlike a global intellectual disability, so 'intellectual disorder' can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because one wrongly frames a learning disability as a mood or feelings problem and the other wrongly equates it with globally low intelligence?

choices.label: A
choices.text: an emotional disorder and an intellectual disorder

choices.label: B
choices.text: a neurological disorder and a developmental disorder

choices.label: C
choices.text: an emotional disorder and a neurological disorder

choices.label: D
choices.text: an intellectual disorder and a developmental disorder

hints.rationale: A learning disability is neither primarily emotional nor a global intellectual deficit, so both can be eliminated.

questions.explanation: Learning disabilities are neurological disorders that affect specific cognitive processes (such as reading or math) and are distinct from overall intelligence.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q23 = B.

hints.prompt: A phonological loop, visuospatial sketchpad, episodic buffer, and central executive are the components of whose model of memory?
hints.rationale: These four components define Baddeley and Hitch's model.

hints.prompt: Sensory memory is best described as which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A very brief store (a fraction of a second to a few seconds) holding raw sensory impressions

choices.label: B
choices.text: A system that actively manipulates information using several subcomponents

choices.label: C
choices.text: A lifelong store of personal life events

choices.label: D
choices.text: Memory for skills such as riding a bike

hints.rationale: Sensory memory is a fleeting raw-input buffer, not the multi-component manipulator described, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because both are LONG-TERM memory types - one for personal events, the other for unconscious skills - rather than an active, temporary workspace?
hints.rationale: Episodic and implicit memory are long-term stores, not the temporary multi-component workspace described.

questions.stem: ________ is a memory store with a phonological loop, visuospatial sketchpad, episodic buffer, and a central executive.
questions.explanation: Baddeley and Hitch's model of working memory comprises the phonological loop, visuospatial sketchpad, episodic buffer, and central executive.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from the live OpenStax page (option C = 'working memory'). The Student Solutions Guide prints the Ch. 8 Q1 answer as 'short-term memory'; the keyed letter C is unchanged and 'working memory' is the correct term for Baddeley's model.

hints.prompt: Memory can be understood as an information-processing system. Getting information INTO memory in the first place is called which process?
hints.rationale: Encoding is the process of getting information into memory.

hints.prompt: In memory research, 'automatic processing' and 'effortful processing' are two kinds of which single stage?
hints.rationale: Automatic and effortful processing are both types of encoding, not distinct memory functions.

hints.prompt: Which two of the main answer choices can be eliminated because they mistakenly list 'automatic processing' and 'effortful processing' as separate memory functions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the two options that begin 'automatic processing, effortful processing'

choices.label: D
choices.text: none of them can be eliminated

hints.rationale: Automatic and effortful processing are subtypes of encoding, not standalone functions, so both options listing them can be eliminated.

questions.stem: The three functions of memory are ________.

choices.label: A
choices.text: automatic processing, effortful processing, and storage

choices.label: C
choices.text: automatic processing, effortful processing, and retrieval

questions.explanation: Memory has three core functions: encoding (getting information in), storage (retaining it), and retrieval (getting it back out).
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 8 Q3 = D.

hints.prompt: The memory described here is tied to emotionally significant or surprising events. Emotional arousal tends to do what to memory for an event?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Strengthen and sharpen the memory of it

choices.label: C
choices.text: Convert the memory into a reflex

choices.label: D
choices.text: Have no effect on the memory at all

hints.rationale: Emotional arousal tends to strengthen memory for an event.

hints.prompt: An 'engram' refers to which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The hypothesized physical trace where a memory is stored in the brain

choices.label: B
choices.text: An unusually vivid memory of a shocking event

choices.label: C
choices.text: A theory about the effects of arousal

choices.label: D
choices.text: The idea that memory is spread across the whole cortex

hints.rationale: An engram is the physical memory trace, not a type of vivid recollection, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Two of the options are names of theories or hypotheses rather than a kind of memory. Which pair are these?

choices.label: A
choices.text: arousal theory and the equipotentiality hypothesis

hints.rationale: 'Arousal theory' and the 'equipotentiality hypothesis' are theoretical proposals, not types of memory, so both can be eliminated.

questions.stem: An exceptionally clear recollection of an important event is a (an) ________.
questions.explanation: A flashbulb memory is an exceptionally vivid, detailed 'snapshot' memory of an emotionally significant or surprising event.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 8 Q5 = C.

hints.prompt: The tip-of-the-tongue experience is an example of which kind of memory problem?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A temporary failure to RETRIEVE information you know is stored

choices.label: D
choices.text: An unwanted memory intruding when you do not want it

hints.rationale: Tip-of-the-tongue is a temporary retrieval failure.

hints.prompt: 'Transience' as a memory sin refers to which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The gradual fading or loss of memories over time

choices.label: B
choices.text: A temporary inability to retrieve information that is still stored

choices.label: C
choices.text: Remembering something but attributing it to the wrong source

choices.label: D
choices.text: The persistent, unwanted return of a memory

hints.rationale: Transience is the fading of memories over time, not a momentary retrieval block, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because one is the intrusive, unwanted return of a memory and the other is assigning a memory to the wrong source?
hints.rationale: Persistence is intrusive recall and misattribution is wrong-source memory, so neither is the temporary word-retrieval block of tip-of-the-tongue.

questions.stem: Tip-of-the-tongue phenomenon is also known as ________.
questions.explanation: The tip-of-the-tongue phenomenon is a form of blocking: the temporary inability to retrieve information that you know is stored in memory.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 8 Q7 = D.

hints.prompt: A phrase or trick designed to help you remember a list is generally called what?
hints.rationale: A memory aid like this is called a mnemonic device.

hints.prompt: An acronym differs from the device in this example in that an acronym does which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Combines the first letters into a single pronounceable WORD, like NASA

choices.label: B
choices.text: Uses the first letters to begin the words of a whole sentence

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sets the information to a melody or tune

choices.label: D
choices.text: Relies on words that sound alike

hints.rationale: An acronym forms a single word from initial letters, unlike a first-letter sentence, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because one is a memory aid set to a tune or melody and the other simply refers to sound?
hints.rationale: A jingle relies on melody and 'acoustic' refers to sound, so neither describes a sentence built from initial letters.

questions.stem: When you are learning how to play the piano, the statement "Every good boy does fine" can help you remember the notes E, G, B, D, and F for the lines of the treble clef. This is an example of a (an) ________.
questions.explanation: An acrostic is a mnemonic in which the first letter of each word in a phrase cues the items to be remembered (here, E-G-B-D-F).
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 8 Q9 = C.

hints.prompt: The self-referencing effect works because relating information to YOURSELF leads to which outcome?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Deeper processing and better memory for that information

hints.rationale: Relating material to yourself produces deeper processing and better recall.

hints.prompt: Which of the following describes an ACROSTIC, as opposed to relating material to yourself?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Making a sentence whose words each begin with the letters to be remembered

choices.label: B
choices.text: Connecting the material to your own personal experiences

choices.label: C
choices.text: Thinking about how the material applies to your own life

choices.label: D
choices.text: Linking new facts to your personal memories

hints.rationale: An acrostic is a first-letter sentence trick, a different strategy from making material personally meaningful, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because one forms a single word from initial letters and the other is simply repeating words aloud - neither relating the material to oneself?

choices.label: A
choices.text: making a word from the first letters and saying words out loud

choices.label: B
choices.text: making the material personally meaningful and relating it to yourself

choices.label: C
choices.text: making a word from first letters and making material personally meaningful

choices.label: D
choices.text: saying words out loud and relating material to yourself

hints.rationale: Forming an acronym and reciting words aloud are impersonal techniques, so neither is the self-referencing effect.

choices.label: A
choices.text: making the material you are trying to memorize personally meaningful to you

choices.label: B
choices.text: making a phrase of all the first letters of the words you are trying to memorize

choices.label: C
choices.text: making a word formed by the first letter of each of the words you are trying to memorize

choices.label: D
choices.text: saying words you want to remember out loud to yourself

questions.explanation: The self-referencing effect is the tendency to remember information better when you relate it to yourself, making the material personally meaningful.

questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 8 Q11 = A.
